title: What Evidence Do I Have That I Belong Here?
subtitle: A journal prompt for finding concrete evidence of belonging when the feeling is absent
excerpt: A journal prompt for the imposter feeling: the sense of not belonging or not having earned your place. The prompt asks for evidence, not for reassurance. It is a different and more useful question.

practice.whenToUse: Use when the imposter feeling is active, or when you need a way to find your own evidence rather than waiting for external validation.

content.text: The imposter feeling is one of the most widespread features of adult professional and creative life. The sense that you do not quite belong in the room you are in, that you have not earned your place, that it is only a matter of time before someone notices. This prompt asks a different question: not whether you feel like you belong, but what the evidence says.
Set a timer for ten minutes. Write without stopping. Begin with the prompt below.
What evidence do I have that I belong here?

The word evidence is deliberate. Not: how do I feel about belonging? Not: reassure me that I belong. Evidence. Specific instances. Things you have done, created, contributed, built, learned, or survived. Facts about your presence in this space that a reasonable person would recognise as evidence of belonging.

If you get stuck, try: What have I done here that was useful? What do I know that took time to learn? What has the presence of my thinking or work produced? What would be missing if I were not here? Who has told me, directly or indirectly, that my being here matters? The answer to these questions exists. Writing it down makes it available to the part of you that tends to forget it when the imposter voice is loudest.
